summary: I came from Afghanistan in 2016 after working there for ten years as an interpreter for the US Army. It was a very dangerous job because interpreters were often targeted. My experience in Afghanistan shaped my commitment to help my country and work alongside the US military.

I worked with the United States Army in Afghanistan as an interpreter for ten years starting in 2006. This role was critical and risky, as interpreters were targeted by enemies. Because of my service with the US Army, I was granted a special immigration visa that allowed me to come to the United States.

After arriving in the US, I ended up living in Pennsylvania where I was given six months' rent and a place to live. I found a job there, but unfortunately, the chemicals in the paint I worked with affected my health badly. This experience was challenging, but it was part of my early life in America.

When my lung tumor worsened, I chose to get treatment in the Washington, D.C. area because I knew someone who could help me. I applied for Medicaid there and underwent many tests before the doctors decided to remove the tumor. I still have regular CT scans in D.C. and sometimes use a breathing machine to help with my condition.

After my health deteriorated due to a lung tumor, I applied for Medicaid to help cover the medical tests and treatment. Medicaid provided support during a difficult time when I needed surgery and ongoing care. This assistance was crucial for managing my health while living in the US.

I was raised in a Muslim family and continue to follow Islam, which taught me values like respecting humanity and doing good work. My parents emphasized that Islam does not condone killing innocents, which aligns with my service for the US Army to help my country. I pray regularly and try to teach my son to be a good person grounded in these beliefs.

In Alexandria, Virginia, there are mosques where my family and I sometimes attend prayers. Occasionally, we have also been invited to pray in a church, which shows the community's inclusiveness. Alexandria has been an important place for our spiritual life since moving to the US.
